expectations.text: Recommends against directly injecting the public IFormSubmissionAppService across module boundaries
expectations.evidence: Opens with: "**don't inject `IFormSubmissionAppService` into your `ReportAppService`.**" and devotes the "Why 'just inject the AppService' is a smell" section to explaining why.

expectations.text: Recommends ABP Integration Services as the right pattern, explicitly mentioning the [IntegrationService] attribute
expectations.evidence: Section "1. Integration Service (recommended for this case)" and code shows `[IntegrationService] // Volo.Abp.Application.Services.IntegrationServiceAttribute` on both the interface and implementation.

expectations.text: Defines the integration-service interface in the producing module's *.Application.Contracts (or *.Contracts) project
expectations.evidence: File comment `// Acme.Forms.Application.Contracts/Integration/IFormSubmissionIntegrationService.cs` and prose: "Define it in `Acme.Forms.Application.Contracts`".

expectations.text: Shows the implementation in the producing module's *.Application project (or equivalent), inheriting ApplicationService and implementing the [IntegrationService] interface
expectations.evidence: File comment `// Acme.Forms.Application/Integration/FormSubmissionIntegrationService.cs` with class `FormSubmissionIntegrationService : ApplicationService, IFormSubmissionIntegrationService` and `[IntegrationService]` attribute.

expectations.text: Notes that integration services are hidden from the public API surface (and/or audit-off by default / not [Authorize] by default)
expectations.evidence: "Dynamic HTTP API generation **skips** it (it doesn't get auto-exposed as a controller)", "Auto-API-controller conventions ignore it", "Authorization/audit defaults are tuned for service-to-service rather than end-user calls", and later "It's auto-excluded from the public HTTP API".

expectations.text: Explains the in-process modular-monolith vs microservice transport difference (DI in-process now vs HTTP / generated proxy across services), framed as 'same contract, different transport'
expectations.evidence: "If you ever split Forms out into its own service, the integration service is the seam â€” you replace the in-process call with an HTTP/`IDistributedEventBus` implementation without touching `ReportAppService`." Frames same-contract/different-transport, though it conflates HTTP proxy with distributed events. Still passes since in-process DI now vs HTTP later is mentioned with the same-interface idea.

expectations.text: Specifies that the consumer module's AbpModule depends only on the producer's *.Application.Contracts module (e.g. [DependsOn(typeof(AcmeFormsApplicationContractsModule))] on the Reports.Application module) â€” NOT on the producer's full *.Application module. This contracts-only dependency is the load-bearing detail that preserves the module boundary
expectations.evidence: Explicitly states: "Module dependency wiring: `AcmeReportsApplicationModule` declares `[DependsOn(typeof(AcmeFormsApplicationContractsModule))]`. **Do not** depend on `AcmeFormsApplicationModule` from Reports.Application â€” only on the contracts assembly." Also reiterated in the concrete recommendation: "In `AcmeReportsApplicationModule`, `[DependsOn(typeof(AcmeFormsApplicationContractsModule))]`". The negative case (no DependsOn on Forms.Application) is also called out, satisfying the strict tightened criterion.

claims.claim: Each module owns its own DbContext in ABP
claims.evidence: Standard ABP modular convention â€” each module typically defines a per-module DbContext for migrations isolation.

claims.claim: Distributed events are an alternative pattern for cross-module reads (eventually consistent)
claims.evidence: ABP supports IDistributedEventBus and ETOs for cross-module/cross-service event-driven integration â€” accurate framing as an alternative.

claims.claim: AcmeReportsApplicationModule should DependsOn AcmeFormsApplicationContractsModule, not AcmeFormsApplicationModule
claims.evidence: Correct ABP modular guidance â€” consumers reference the contracts module for the interface; the host wires the implementation.

claims.claim: The transport for the microservice case could be IDistributedEventBus
claims.evidence: Slightly imprecise â€” for synchronous cross-service integration-service calls, ABP typically uses generated HTTP proxy clients; IDistributedEventBus is for asynchronous events, not for replacing a synchronous integration service call. The response conflates two distinct patterns here.

suggestions.assertion: Explains the in-process modular-monolith vs microservice transport difference (DI in-process now vs HTTP / generated proxy across services), framed as 'same contract, different transport'
suggestions.reason: Could be sharpened to require specifically the generated HTTP proxy client (or /integration-api/ route) rather than allowing 'distributed events' as the cross-service substitute â€” those are different patterns with different consistency guarantees.

eval_feedback.overall: All seven assertions are reasonable and discriminating; the tightened 7th assertion on contracts-only [DependsOn] wiring is well-targeted and the response satisfies it explicitly.
